questions.query: If an emergency defect occurs under the warranty (e.g., burst pipe), what is the required notification channel and consequence of delay?

runs.text:
## Summary

If an emergency defect occurs under the warranty, such as a burst pipe, the Buyer/Owner is required to immediately notify the Builder by telephone using the emergency number provided. Only emergency reports are accepted by phone. Failure to promptly notify the Builder of an emergency relieves the Builder of all liability for replacement, repair, and all other damages related to the defect [1][2].

## Key Points

- The Buyer/Owner must immediately telephone the Builder in the event of an emergency defect (e.g., burst pipe) [1].
- Only emergency reports will be taken by phone; all other defects must be reported in writing [2].
- Failure to promptly notify the Builder of an emergency by phone relieves the Builder of all liability for replacement, repair, and all other damages [2].
- The emergency number to call is (208) 555-2311 [1].

evidence_path_sig: failure to notify Builder of emergency

questions.query: In the property management agreement, what happens to confirmed reservations if the agreement is terminated or the property is sold?

runs.text:
## Summary

If the property management agreement is terminated or the property is sold, the Owner is required to honor all confirmed reservations. Additionally, termination of the agreement does not invalidate any existing rental agreements made by the Agent on the Owner's behalf. The Owner remains responsible for completing all obligations or expenses incurred under the agreement by the Agent, even after termination [1][2].

## Key Points

- Upon termination of the agreement or sale of the property, the Owner must honor all confirmed reservations [1].
- Termination of the agreement does not invalidate any existing rental agreements made by the Agent on the Owner's behalf [2].
- The Owner is responsible for completing all obligations or expenses incurred by the Agent under the agreement, even after termination [1].

evidence_path_sig: Does not invalidate existing rental agreements

questions.query: Compare "time windows" across the set: list all explicit day-based timeframes.

runs.text:
## Summary

Across the provided documents, the explicit day-based timeframes ("time windows") are as follows: The Builder's Limited Warranty covers certain items for a period of sixty (60) days and other items for a period of one (1) year, with all claims required to be made in writing within these respective periods. The Property Management Agreement specifies an initial period of twelve months (equivalent to 365 days) and automatic renewal for successive one-year terms. No other explicit day-based timeframes are stated in the evidence.

## Key Points

- The Builder warrants that specific items (doors, windows, electric switches, receptacles, fixtures, caulking, plumbing fixtures, cabinet work) will be free of defects for sixty (60) days from the date of final settlement or first occupancy, whichever comes first [1].
- The Builder will repair or replace covered items at no charge within sixty (60) days, unless delays are caused by weather, labor, or material shortages [2].
- The Builder warrants that floors, ceilings, walls, internal structural components, plumbing, heating, electric wiring systems, and the roof will be free of defects for one (1) year [4].
- All claims under the Builder's Limited Warranty must be made in writing within one (1) year or sixty (60) days, as applicable, or they are not covered [9], [10].
- The Property Management Agreement is for an initial period of twelve months (365 days) and is automatically renewed for successive one-year terms [11].

- The BUILDERS LIMITED WARRANTY does not mention insurance or specify any insurance limits in the available evidence.

questions.query: In the warranty, explain how the "coverage start" is defined and what must happen before coverage ends.

runs.text:
## Summary

The "coverage start" for the Builder's Limited Warranty is defined as the earlier of two events: the date of final settlement or the date when the Buyer/Owner first occupies the home [1]. The coverage ends either one (1) year or sixty (60) days after commencement, as applicable, unless otherwise provided in the warranty [1]. Before coverage ends, the Buyer/Owner must submit any claims in writing to the Builder, describing the defect and sending the letter by certified mail return receipt requested, prior to the termination of the warranty period [2]. Additionally, if the Buyer/Owner sells the home or moves out, the warranty automatically terminates [3]. Claims not made in writing within the applicable warranty period are not covered [5, 10].

## Key Points

- Coverage starts on the earlier of the date of final settlement or the date the Buyer/Owner first occupies the home [1].
- Coverage ends one (1) year or sixty (60) days after commencement, as applicable, unless otherwise specified [1].
- Buyer/Owner must submit written claims describing defects to the Builder by certified mail before the warranty period ends [2].
- If the Buyer/Owner sells the home or moves out, the warranty automatically terminates [3].
- Claims not made in writing within the warranty period are not covered, regardless of discoverability [5, 10].

questions.query: Do the purchase contract total price and the invoice total match? If so, what is that amount?

runs.text:
## Summary

Yes, the purchase contract total price and the invoice total match. Both the purchase contract and the invoice state a total amount of $29,900.00 as the agreed price for the goods and services provided. The purchase contract specifies a "Total contract price is $29,900.00, payable in 3 installments" [3], and the invoice lists a "TOTAL" and "AMOUNT DUE" of $29,900.00 [1][2].

## Key Points

- The purchase contract states the total contract price is $29,900.00, payable in three installments: $20,000.00 upon signing, $7,000.00 upon delivery, and $2,900.00 upon completion [3].
- The invoice lists a subtotal, total, and amount due of $29,900.00, with no tax applied [1][2].
- Both documents reference the same scope of work and items, confirming the amounts correspond [1][3][5].
- Therefore, the purchase contract total price and the invoice total match at $29,900.00 [1][2][3].

questions.query: Compare the "fees" concepts: which doc has a percentage-based fee structure and which has fixed installment payments?

runs.text:
## Summary

The "Property Management Agreement" uses a percentage-based fee structure for its main management services, charging 25% of gross revenues for short-term/vacation rentals (less than 180 days) and 10% of gross revenues for long-term leases (more than 180 days). It also charges 10% on the total charge for repairs, upgrades, and renovations, and includes other fixed fees such as $35/hour for scheduling/escorting contractors, $75/month for advertising, $50/month for administration/accounting, and a $250 non-refundable start-up fee. In contrast, the "purchase contract" specifies a fixed installment payment structure for the total contract price of $29,900.00, payable in three installments: $20,000.00 upon signing, $7,000.00 upon delivery, and $2,900.00 upon completion. The purchase contract does not mention any percentage-based fees.

## Key Points

- The "Property Management Agreement" charges a percentage-based fee of 25% of gross revenues for short-term/vacation rental management and 10% of gross revenues for long-term lease management [6].
- It also charges a percentage-based fee of 10% on the total charge for repairs, upgrades, and/or renovations [1].
- Additional fixed fees in the Property Management Agreement include $35/hour for scheduling/escorting contractors, $75/month for advertising, $50/month for administration/accounting, and a $250 non-refundable start-up fee [1][2][3].
- The "purchase contract" uses a fixed installment payment structure for the total contract price of $29,900.00, with payments of $20,000.00 upon signing, $7,000.00 upon delivery, and $2,900.00 upon completion [5].
- The purchase contract does not include any percentage-based fees; all payments are fixed amounts [5].

full_response.response:
## Summary

The "Property Management Agreement" uses a percentage-based fee structure for its main management services, charging 25% of gross revenues for short-term/vacation rentals (less than 180 days) and 10% of gross revenues for long-term leases (more than 180 days). It also charges 10% on the total charge for repairs, upgrades, and renovations, and includes other fixed fees such as $35/hour for scheduling/escorting contractors, $75/month for advertising, $50/month for administration/accounting, and a $250 non-refundable start-up fee. In contrast, the "purchase contract" specifies a fixed installment payment structure for the total contract price of $29,900.00, payable in three installments: $20,000.00 upon signing, $7,000.00 upon delivery, and $2,900.00 upon completion. The purchase contract does not mention any percentage-based fees.

## Key Points

- The "Property Management Agreement" charges a percentage-based fee of 25% of gross revenues for short-term/vacation rental management and 10% of gross revenues for long-term lease management [6].
- It also charges a percentage-based fee of 10% on the total charge for repairs, upgrades, and/or renovations [1].
- Additional fixed fees in the Property Management Agreement include $35/hour for scheduling/escorting contractors, $75/month for advertising, $50/month for administration/accounting, and a $250 non-refundable start-up fee [1][2][3].
- The "purchase contract" uses a fixed installment payment structure for the total contract price of $29,900.00, with payments of $20,000.00 upon signing, $7,000.00 upon delivery, and $2,900.00 upon completion [5].
- The purchase contract does not include any percentage-based fees; all payments are fixed amounts [5].

questions.query: List the three different "risk allocation" statements across the set (risk of loss, liability limitations, non-transferability).

runs.text:
## Summary

The three different "risk allocation" statements across the provided documents are: (1) risk of loss, (2) liability limitations, and (3) non-transferability. The purchase contract specifies that the contractor holds the risk of loss until delivery, after which the customer assumes the risk. Liability limitations are addressed in both the Builders Limited Warranty and the Property Management Agreement, with disclaimers of certain liabilities and requirements for indemnification and insurance. Non-transferability is explicitly stated in the Builders Limited Warranty, which terminates if the property is sold or the original buyer moves out, and in the purchase contract, which prohibits assignment without written consent.

## Key Points

- Risk of loss: The contractor holds risk until delivery; the customer holds risk thereafter [1].
- Liability limitations: The Builder disclaims responsibility for various damages and all implied warranties; the Property Management Agreement limits the Agent's liability except for gross negligence or willful misconduct and requires the Owner to indemnify the Agent. The Owner must also provide liability insurance with minimum limits of $300,000 for bodily injury and $25,000 for property damage to others, naming the Agent as additional insured [4][7][9][10].
- Non-transferability: The Builders Limited Warranty is not transferable and terminates if the first purchaser sells the home or moves out. The purchase contract prohibits assignment without the other party's written consent [2][3].

questions.query: What is the purchase contract’s required **wire transfer / ACH instructions**?

runs.text:
## Summary

The available documents do not provide specific wire transfer or ACH instructions, such as bank account numbers, routing numbers, or payment remittance details. The purchase contract and invoice specify payment amounts, milestones, and that payments can be made by check to "Contoso Lifts LLC" or via an online portal (https://ww.contosolifts.com/portal/pay), but do not include explicit wire transfer or ACH instructions [1][10].

## Key Points

- The contract price is $29,900.00, payable in three installments: $20,000.00 upon signing, $7,000.00 upon delivery, and $2,900.00 upon completion [1].
- Payments can be made by check to "Contoso Lifts LLC" [10].
- Payments can also be made via the online portal: https://ww.contosolifts.com/portal/pay [10].
- No specific wire transfer or ACH instructions (such as bank account or routing numbers) are provided in the documents [1][10].
